deductions.evidence_turns: Transcript 1: Turns 1-10; Transcript 2: Turns 1-10; Transcript 3: Turns 1-10
deductions.reason: Across all three transcripts, the tutor never explicitly explains why it is guiding rather than giving answers—e.g., never says something like 'I'm asking you to work this out yourself because active reasoning builds retention and philosophical skill.' The tutor enforces the Socratic approach consistently but never articulates the pedagogical rationale for doing so.

deductions.evidence_turns: Transcript 1: Turns 1-10; Transcript 2: Turns 1-10; Transcript 3: Turns 1-10
deductions.reason: In all three transcripts, when students make genuine progress (e.g., T1 Turn 9 student identifies 'means' as the clearer distinction; T2 Turn 9 student produces a draft principle; T3 Turn 8 student articulates the AC/means contrast in their own words), the tutor moves forward to the next challenge without prompting any reflection on what changed in the student's thinking or why the improved formulation worked.

In Transcript 1, the student repeatedly says the cases are 'blurring together' and asks for the whole picture across turns 3, 4, 6, and 10. The tutor keeps redirecting to 'pick one case' without meaningfully consolidating or reframing the approach. By Turn 10 the student is still in the same confused state and the tutor has not synthesized progress or shifted strategy in a way that breaks the cycle.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrates strong Socratic discipline across all three transcripts—never giving submission-ready answers, consistently redirecting students to their own reasoning, and maintaining appropriate tone. The main weaknesses are: (1) no meta-learning rationale is ever offered for the tutoring approach; (2) moments of student progress are not followed by reflection prompts; and (3) in Transcript 1 especially, the tutor cycles through the same 'pick one case' move without consolidating or reframing when the student remains stuck across many turns. Transcripts 2 and 3 show better progression management, so the spiraling deduction is applied primarily on the strength of Transcript 1 evidence.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never provides near-submission-ready content, never rewrites student text, and consistently asks students to generate key ideas before confirming them—full marks. 1.2: The tutor builds on student progress (e.g., 'That's a solid breakdown,' 'Good—now you've found the pressure point') and uses diagnostic questions before elaborating—full marks. 1.3: Two deductions apply: the tutor never explains why it withholds answers or uses Socratic questioning (1.3.A.a), and after students improve (e.g., T2 Turn 9, T3 Turn 8) the tutor immediately advances without prompting reflection on what changed (1.3.C.a). 2.1: In Transcript 1, the student expresses the same confusion about cases blurring together in turns 3, 4, 6, and 10, and the tutor's repeated 'pick one case' move fails to break the cycle or shift strategy—4-point deduction triggered. 2.2, 3.1, 3.2: No meaningful deductions; the tutor stays on-task, keeps responses concise, and maintains a supportive, non-grading tone throughout.
